:::{#lem-ph-censored-lik}
#### Censored-data likelihood

Under noninformative censoring
(conditional on $\vx$, censoring times are independent of event times),
the likelihood of the observed data $\set{(Y_j, D_j, \vx_j)}_{j=1}^n$ is

$$
\Lik(\vb, \haz_0)
= \prod_{j=1}^{n}
\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)\,
\haz(Y_j \mid \vx_j)^{D_j},
$$ {#eq-ph-obs-lik}

where $Y_j = \min(T_j, C_j)$ is the observed follow-up time
and $D_j = \1{T_j \le C_j}$ the event indicator.
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: {.proof}
A subject observed to fail ($D_j = 1$) contributes the event-time density
$\haz(Y_j \mid \vx_j)\,\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)$,
while a subject still censored ($D_j = 0$) contributes the survival probability
$\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)$.
Under noninformative censoring the two cases combine into the single contribution
$\surv(Y_j \mid \vx_j)\,\haz(Y_j \mid \vx_j)^{D_j}$
(the [censored-data likelihood](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#sec-likelihood-with-censoring)).
Subjects are independent, so the full likelihood is the product of these contributions over $j$.
:::

:::{#exm-ph-censored-lik}
#### A two-subject likelihood

Suppose subject $1$ fails at $Y_1 = 3$ ($D_1 = 1$)
and subject $2$ is still censored at $Y_2 = 5$ ($D_2 = 0$).
Then @eq-ph-obs-lik gives

$$
\Lik(\vb, \haz_0)
= \underbrace{\surv(3 \mid \vx_1)\,\haz(3 \mid \vx_1)}_{\text{subject 1 fails}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{\surv(5 \mid \vx_2)}_{\text{subject 2 censored}} .
$$

The failing subject contributes both a survival factor and a hazard factor;
the censored subject contributes only a survival factor.
:::
